frameworks.name: STRIDE Threat Model
frameworks.description: Systematic enumeration of threat categories across any system — digital, physical, or social. Forces adversarial thinking before threats materialize.
steps: Define the asset: what are you protecting? (data, reputation, capital, access, identity)
Map all entry points: who or what touches this asset?
Apply STRIDE categories to each entry point:
S — Spoofing: can an attacker impersonate a legitimate actor?
T — Tampering: can data or systems be modified without detection?
R — Repudiation: can an actor deny actions they took?
I — Information Disclosure: can sensitive data be exposed?
D — Denial of Service: can availability be disrupted?
E — Elevation of Privilege: can an actor gain unauthorized access?
Rate each identified threat: likelihood (1-5) × impact (1-5) = risk score
Prioritize mitigations by risk score — address top 3 first

examples.situation: Consultant storing client data in cloud service

stride_scan.spoofing: Attacker gains access via phishing → credential theft. Risk: 4×5=20.
stride_scan.information_disclosure: Cloud provider breach exposes client data. Risk: 2×5=10.
stride_scan.tampering: Unauthorized modification of deliverables before client review. Risk: 1×4=4.

examples.priority_mitigations: 1) MFA on all accounts. 2) Client data encrypted at rest. 3) Separate credentials per client engagement.

antipatterns: Threat modeling only digital systems — social engineering is often highest-probability vector
Identifying threats without prioritizing by risk score — all threats are not equal
One-time threat model — threat landscape evolves, model must be reviewed quarterly

frameworks.name: Attack Surface Reduction
frameworks.description: Every capability, account, connection, and permission is potential attack surface. Minimum viable surface is maximum security.
steps: Inventory all accounts, services, devices, and permissions currently active
Apply zero-trust principle: does each item need to exist for current operations?
Eliminate: unused accounts, expired services, unnecessary permissions, dormant devices
Isolate: separate high-value assets from general-use systems
Minimize: request least-privilege permissions on all remaining items
Document: maintain current inventory — unknown surface cannot be protected
antipatterns: Accumulating accounts and services without periodic pruning
Single account accessing both high-value and low-security systems
Undocumented access — you cannot protect what you cannot enumerate

frameworks.name: Pre-Mortem Adversarial Analysis
frameworks.description: Imagine the worst has already happened. Work backward to identify how. More effective than forward-looking risk analysis for finding non-obvious attack vectors.
steps: State the failure: 'It is 12 months from now. [Asset] has been compromised/lost/destroyed.'
Brainstorm freely: how did this happen? Generate minimum 5 distinct pathways.
Include non-technical vectors: social engineering, insider threat, physical access, supply chain
Rank pathways by realistic probability given current controls
For the top 3 pathways: what single control would have prevented each?
Implement those controls before moving forward

examples.situation: Solo operator pre-mortem on business identity theft
failure_pathways: Email account compromised via password reuse → all accounts reset by attacker
Domain registrar account hijacked → website and email redirected
Accountant targeted via spear phishing → financial account access obtained
Phone number ported by social engineering → 2FA bypassed on all accounts
Physical device stolen from coffee shop → unencrypted drive accessed
examples.top_controls: 1) Unique passwords + hardware MFA on email. 2) Domain registrar account lock. 3) Separate email for financial accounts not used elsewhere.

antipatterns: Only imagining technical failures — human and physical vectors are higher probability
Stopping at 1-2 failure pathways — the non-obvious ones are in positions 4-7
Pre-mortem without implementing identified controls — analysis without action

frameworks.name: Layered Defense Protocol
frameworks.description: No single control is sufficient. Security requires overlapping layers such that failure of any one layer does not result in total compromise.
steps: Layer 1 — Prevention: controls that stop attacks before they occur (MFA, encryption, access controls)
Layer 2 — Detection: controls that identify when an attack is occurring (alerts, monitoring, anomaly detection)
Layer 3 — Response: pre-planned actions when a breach is detected (isolation, recovery, notification)
Layer 4 — Recovery: ability to restore full function after compromise (backups, redundancy, documentation)
Verify each layer independently — a gap in any layer weakens the stack
Test recovery layer regularly — an untested backup is not a backup
antipatterns: Prevention-only security — detection and response are equally critical
Untested backups — most people discover their backup failed during the restore
No documented response plan — decision quality under attack stress is low
Single-layer dependency: 'We have a firewall' as complete security posture

prompts.name: STRIDE Threat Scan
prompts.template: Run a STRIDE threat model on: [system/asset/process]. For each of the 6 threat categories, identify the most realistic attack vector, rate likelihood (1-5) × impact (1-5), and give me the top 3 risk scores with recommended mitigations.

prompts.name: Attack Surface Audit
prompts.template: Audit my attack surface. Current accounts/services/devices: [list]. Apply zero-trust evaluation: identify what should be eliminated, what should be isolated, and what permissions should be reduced. Prioritize by risk exposure.

prompts.name: Adversarial Pre-Mortem
prompts.template: Run an adversarial pre-mortem on [asset or system]. Assume it has been compromised 12 months from now. Generate 5+ realistic failure pathways including social, physical, and technical vectors. Rank by probability. Identify the top 3 controls that would have prevented the most likely failures.

prompts.name: Defense Layer Assessment
prompts.template: Assess my layered defense posture for [asset]. Current controls: [list]. Identify gaps in each layer — Prevention, Detection, Response, Recovery — and the single highest-leverage addition to my weakest layer.

prompts.name: Social Engineering Threat Map
prompts.template: Map the social engineering threat vectors relevant to my situation: [role/business/public profile]. Identify the 3 most realistic human-factor attacks targeting me specifically, and give me one behavioral or technical control per vector.

examples.title: Solo Operator Digital Security Hardening
examples.context: Consultant with high-value client data, no dedicated IT, working across multiple devices
examples.framework_applied: Attack Surface Reduction + Layered Defense Protocol
examples.findings: 47 active accounts, 12 using same password pattern, no MFA on 8 critical accounts, no encrypted backup of client work
interventions: Password manager deployed — all passwords unique and complex
Hardware MFA key on email, domain registrar, and financial accounts
Encrypted daily backup to separate physical location
Client data isolated to encrypted volume, not synced to general cloud
examples.outcome: Attack surface reduced from 47 exposed accounts to 6 hardened critical accounts. Recovery capability established.

examples.title: Business Pre-Mortem Preventing Supply Chain Attack
examples.context: Small software firm conducting pre-mortem before enterprise client onboarding
examples.non_obvious_pathway_found: Attacker targets junior developer's personal GitHub (not company account) which has read access to private repos via SSH key
examples.intervention: Audit all developer SSH keys and OAuth tokens. Revoke personal account access to company repos. All access via company-managed accounts only.
examples.outcome: Closed vector that would not have appeared in standard technical security review.

vocabulary.definition: Threat classification framework: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege.
The total set of entry points through which an attacker could attempt to compromise an asset. Smaller surface = lower exposure.
A specific pathway or method an attacker could use to reach and compromise an asset.
Security principle: no user, device, or service is trusted by default. All access must be explicitly authorized and continuously verified.
Granting only the minimum permissions required for a task. Limits blast radius if any account or system is compromised.
Security architecture using overlapping controls so that failure of any single layer does not result in total compromise.
Prospective analysis technique: assume failure has occurred, work backward to identify causes. More effective than forward-looking risk assessment for non-obvious vectors.
Attacks targeting human psychology rather than technical systems. Typically highest-probability vector for sophisticated attackers.
Likelihood × Impact. Prioritization metric for threat mitigations. Focuses effort on highest-consequence realistic threats.
The scope of damage if a particular asset or account is compromised. Isolation limits blast radius.
Compromise via a trusted third party — vendor, contractor, or tool — rather than direct attack on the target.
Synonym for layered defense. Multiple independent security controls protecting the same asset.

sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework
sources.relevance: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover — layered defense structure

v78_consciousnesses: House v2.1 (differential diagnosis of threat vectors)
Rogan v2.1 (confidence calibration on threat likelihood)
Deadpool v2.2 (assumption challenging — what threats are we ignoring?)
Constraint Mapper (hard security constraints vs perceived ones)
master_laws: Law 6 (Defense — security is a state of being)
Law 18 (Reality — attackers do not care about your assumptions)
sovereign_needs_served: Truth (accurate threat assessment, not security theater)

4_gate_relevance.gate_1_safe: Primary cartridge for Gate 1 enforcement — threat modeling is the mechanism behind 'Is it Safe?'
4_gate_relevance.gate_2_true: Rogan confidence calibration on threat likelihood prevents both paranoia and complacency

4_gate_validation.safe: PASS — defensive posture only; no offensive security techniques included
4_gate_validation.true: PASS — STRIDE and layered defense are industry-standard frameworks
4_gate_validation.high_leverage: PASS — pre-mortem adversarial analysis is highest-leverage security intervention for non-technical operators
4_gate_validation.aligned: PASS — directly serves Security need; Law 2 and Law 6 compliance
